title: GTPCNZ
text:
GTPCNZ is a public policy-research and translation package on primary care funding architecture in Aotearoa New Zealand and Australia.
Website: https://edithatogo.github.io/gtpcnz/



A source-informed model scaffold for exploring primary care funding architecture.
A Quarto website and reproducible report.
A Streamlit dashboard for interactive scenario exploration.
A public audit trail of assumptions, caveats, and launch materials.





It is not an endorsed policy position.
It is not a real-data calibrated forecasting model.
It should not be used to claim precise fiscal savings, hospital-demand reductions, or workforce effects.




Capitation should be retained for continuity, enrolment, baseline viability, and population responsibility. Eligible primary medical activity should also have an uncapped, scheduled, rules-based fee-for-service stream, controlled through item rules, provider scope, clinical governance, documentation, audit, co-payment protections, and place-based accountability.
Short version:

Uncapped does not mean uncontrolled.





Quarto website: https://edithatogo.github.io/gtpcnz/
Quarto source report: reports/primary_care_architecture.qmd
Streamlit dashboard entrypoint: streamlit_app.py
Dashboard implementation: models/primarycare_model/app.py
Model card: docs/calibration/model-card-v1.7.2.md
Claim boundaries: docs/launch/claim-boundaries-v1.7.2.md




Install dependencies:
pip install -r requirements.txt
Run tests:
pytest -q
Render the Quarto website:
quarto render --to html
Run the Streamlit dashboard:
streamlit run streamlit_app.py



GitHub Pages is deployed from .github/workflows/pages.yml.
Streamlit Community Cloud can deploy this app with:

Repository: edithatogo/gtpcnz
Branch: main
Entrypoint: streamlit_app.py




See LICENSE for the mixed code/content licensing terms and CITATION.cff for citation metadata.

title: GTPCNZ Interactive Reporting & Visualization Suite
text:
This project includes a state-of-the-art (SOTA) reporting layer and an interactive dashboard to make the primary care funding model accessible to both policy experts and citizens (calibrated for a 14-year-old audience).


The Quarto report is a reproducible document that combines technical thesis writing with live Python data analysis.


To generate the HTML or PDF version, ensure you have Quarto installed and run:
quarto render reports/primary_care_architecture.qmd




Narrative first: Explains the “Game Theory” of healthcare using relatable metaphors (e.g., video games, subscription services).
Embedded Evidence: Pulls directly from outputs/full-parameterised-summary-results-v1.7.0.csv.





“GTPCNZ” is an interactive tool allowing users to simulate the impact of different funding rules.


Ensure you have the dependencies installed:
pip install -r requirements.txt
Then launch the dashboard:
streamlit run streamlit_app.py




Interactive Sliders: Adjust Capitation vs. FFS weights.
Educational Tooltips: Instant definitions for complex healthcare terms.
Real-time Plotting: Immediate feedback on how your settings impact “Doctor Supply” and “Hospital Pressure”.
Deployment-ready Entry Point: streamlit_app.py is the Streamlit Community Cloud entrypoint.






Modularity: The dashboard leverages the existing project data structure.
Accessibility: Content is simplified without losing technical rigor.
Transparency: All visualisations are generated from traceable project outputs.
Automated Testing: models/tests/test_app.py uses Streamlit’s native AppTest API.

text:
Local Commands
Render the website:
quarto render
Run the dashboard:

title: GTPCNZ: The Funding Architecture Report
text:
Executive Summary
Imagine you are playing a video game where you have to keep a city healthy. If you only pay the doctors when they work in the big, expensive hospitals, but don’t pay them enough to work in the small neighborhood clinics, what happens? People get sick in their neighborhoods and then rush to the hospital all at once. The hospital gets crowded, and everyone has to wait a long time.
This report explains a new way to fund (pay for) primary care—the doctors and nurses in your neighborhood. We want to make sure they are paid fairly for every person they help, so that they can stop health problems before they become big enough to need a hospital visit.


1. The Big Idea: “Uncapped but Controlled”
Right now, funding for doctors can be like a “fixed envelope” of money. Once that envelope is empty, it’s hard for doctors to help more people without losing money themselves.
Our proposal is different: “Uncapped at the activity-envelope level, but controlled at the item level.”

What does this mean?

Uncapped: There is no fixed limit on how many people a doctor can help. If someone is sick, the doctor should be able to help them and be paid for that work.
Controlled: Just because there is no limit doesn’t mean there are no rules. We use strict rules (like a rulebook in a game) to make sure the right care is given to the right people by the right providers.




3. What is “Capitation”?
In healthcare, Capitation is like a subscription service for your doctor.
Imagine you pay a monthly fee to a music streaming app. You can listen to as many songs as you want, and the app gets the same amount of money whether you listen to 1 song or 1,000 songs.

The Good Side: The doctor has a steady income and knows they are responsible for your health all year round.
The Bad Side: If 1,000 people all want an appointment at the same time, the doctor might not have enough money (or staff) to see everyone, because the “subscription fee” doesn’t increase when the work increases.



4. Results: Why the “Full Hybrid” Model Wins
We tested 10 different ways to pay for healthcare. Here is how they compare in our simulation.










Key Findings:

Full Hybrid Upstream Architecture (Winner): This model combines the “subscription” (Capitation) with a “pay-per-visit” (FFS) system. It makes it easier for you to see a doctor and reduces the pressure on big hospitals.
Current Reform Pathway: While it makes some improvements, it doesn’t do as much to help doctors offer more appointments when demand is high.




5. Conclusion
By changing the rules of the “funding game,” we can make sure that your neighborhood doctor is there when you need them. This doesn’t just help you; it helps the whole country by keeping our big hospitals free for real emergencies.

Technical Note: This report was automatically generated using Quarto. The data comes from the full-parameterised-summary-results-v1.7.0.csv file in the project outputs.

title: Streamlit Deployment
text:
GTPCNZ is prepared for Streamlit Community Cloud.


Use:
streamlit_app.py
The root entrypoint imports and runs models.primarycare_model.app.render_app.




requirements.txt declares Python dependencies.
.streamlit/config.toml stores public app configuration.
.streamlit/secrets.toml.example documents that no secrets are required.
models/tests/test_app.py uses streamlit.testing.v1.AppTest for headless dashboard checks.




pip install -r requirements.txt
pytest -q models/tests/test_app.py
streamlit run streamlit_app.py




Repository: edithatogo/gtpcnz
Branch: main
Main file path: streamlit_app.py
Python version: 3.11




The dashboard is a demonstrative simulator. It is not a real-data calibrated policy forecast.
